story:
Rain began to fall. The narrow streets were dim and unfamiliar. Maryellen moved through them in the dream as if she were following someone else’s fear. Shops she knew—the bakery, the shoe repair—were dark, though a cracked neon sign still blinked. On the next block, a vacant lot gaped where buildings should have been. No bus stop in sight, no people, not even a stray dog. Cars hissed by, soaking his shoes. He was cold. He sped up. Unease rose: someone was behind him. He stopped to listen. Nothing. He walked on, but the feeling stayed.

In the dream she watched Keith turn a corner and tuck himself behind a Salvation Army donation bin. Still no footsteps, only rain. When he started again, she glimpsed a small figure in dark clothes—maybe a kid. If the kid had a weapon, he could be in trouble. He was strong for forty-four, but fear gnawed at him. At the end of the street he looked back. The figure stood at the far corner, then closed the distance quickly. He ran until his side ached. He bent over to breathe—and saw two black shoes right behind him.

It was a kid, short and slim. A black scarf hid the face, only the eyes showing—dark and wide against the whites. A gun glinted inches away. Keith’s expression changed; even in the dream Maryellen knew the gun was his Glock.

“What do you want?” he managed.

No answer. A twitch of the gun: keep walking. Keith obeyed, mind racing. In the dream, Maryellen didn’t hear his thoughts, only saw his confusion. But there it was—in the kid’s hand. Was this a nightmare?

They turned into an alley ending at a brick wall. Keith faced the figure, trying to stay calm.

“What do you want?” he asked again. Silence. “Who are you?”

Clouds thinned; the full moon slid free.

“Do you want money? Can I take out my wallet?” Slowly, he showed forty dollars. “If we find an ATM, I can get more.”

The kid didn’t move.

“Come on, we can work this out. Do you understand me?”

Still silence. Then the scarf came down.

“Jesus! You? Maryellen?” He stared. “Maryellen! What are you doing? What’s the matter with you?” His tone hardened. He stepped forward, hands jerking toward the gun—toward her throat.

She had never fired a gun. She squeezed the trigger. The first shot sang past his shoulder and chipped the wall. He froze.

“Christ! Maryellen! What’s gotten into you?”

“Scum,” she whispered, steadying the Glock with both hands. He understood then. He begged. He cried. She watched. She fired again—his shoulder. Then his leg. He fell, pain flooding him, tears mixing with rain and grit. He pleaded. Another shot. His breath turned ragged. She came close, knelt, felt his breath on her cheek, and fired into his head. Blood and brain burst—

—and she woke up.

The full moon stared through the bedroom window. Maryellen lay still. Beside her, Keith snored on his back, sprawling across the mattress as if he owned it. She barely looked at him. She was thinking about his gun—the one with the scratch. It felt like a key to her freedom. But it was locked away, and the key hung on a chain around his neck.

She touched a fresh bruise on her shoulder, red and swollen. He had hit her with his belt. Older scars made a grim map. The hidden ones were worse. The first time, she had called the police. It hadn’t mattered. Keith was a captain. His brothers in blue had closed ranks. They had not taken her side. He had told her running was useless; he would find her anywhere, and the punishment would be worse. She looked at his sleeping face.

Scum, she thought, echoing the dream.

She rose and went to the kitchen, too afraid to run the coffee machine. She couldn’t live like this. If only she could reach the gun. She couldn’t. Her eyes slid to the wooden block by the sink. A carving knife gleamed. Slowly, carefully, she pulled it free. It felt huge in her small hands. She gripped it with both. Quietly—very quietly—she stepped back into the bedroom, her choice born of fear and of every failure around her, the ending still unwritten.
